coredata.dc:title: A smart imprinted fluorescence sensor of biomass carbon dots based on magnetic covalent organic frameworks for real-time and sensitive detection of 4-nitrophenol in food

Han, Shuang
Sun, Ruonan

Zhang, Xin
Zhang, Xinyuan
Wang, Yuan
Zhai, Minghui
coredata.dc:description: Developing sensitive and portable methods for detecting 4-nitrophenol (4-NP) in food is important for ensuring food safety. Herein, a novel detection system using magnetic covalent organic frameworks (MCOFs) combined with biomass carbon dots (CDs) embedded in molecularly imprinted polymers (CDs-MFMIPs) has been designed specifically for detecting 4-NP in food. The biomass CDs synthesized from green persimmons serve as the fluorescent source, while the MCOFs enhance the response signals. The synergistic effect of the fluorescence response from the biomass CDs and the specificity of imprinting sites enable excellent 4-NP detection. This ultrasensitive fluorescence probe exhibits rapid response, excellent selectivity, magnetic properties, and a wide linear range of 0.05–50 μM, with a low detection limit of 17.44 nM. Furthermore, the smartphone-assisted sensor allows for convenient, visual, and precise 4-NP detection. This approach offers a reliable method and promising applications for onsite detection of 4-NP in food.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Molecularly imprinted fluorescence sensor
Magnetic covalent organic frameworks
4-nitrophenol
Smartphone platform
Food samples

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Smartphone based sensing device for visual 4-NP detection
Smartphone-assisted portable device for 4-NP detection in food

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study was funded by the Basic Research Program for Excellent Young Teachers in Heilongjiang Provincial Universities ( YQJH2023101 ), Ecological Environment Protection Project of Heilongjiang Province (HST2023QT002), Innovation Incentive Project of Qiqihar Technology Program (CSFGG-2023283) and the Fundamental Research Funds in Heilongjiang Provincial Universities (No: 135409101).

This study was funded by the Program for Young Talents of Basic Research in Universities of Heilongjiang Province (YQJH2023101), Ecological Environment Protection Project of Heilongjiang Province (HST2023QT002), Innovation Incentive Project of Qiqihar Technology Program (CSFGG-2023283) and the Fundamental Research Funds in Heilongjiang Provincial Universities (No: 135409101).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of (A) Fe3O4, (B) MCOFs, (C) CDs-MFMIPs (insert: SEM images) and (D) CDs (insert: particle size distribution of CDs).

(A) XRD patterns of Fe3O4 (a), MCOFs (b), CDs (c) and CDs-MFMIPs (d). (B) FT-IR spectra of CDs (a), Fe3O4 (b), MCOFs (c), CDs-MFMIPs (d), and CDs-MFMIPs + 4-NP (e). (C) Hysteresis loops of Fe3O4, MCOFs, and CDs-MFMIPs (insert: magnetic response of CDs-MFMIPs). (D) TGA curves of MCOFs and CDs-MFMIPs. (E) XPS survey spectra of Fe3O4, MCOFs, and CDs-MFMIPs. (F) High-resolution XPS spectra of CDs-MFMIPs at C 1 s.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of various materials. (B) Excitation and emission spectra of CDs-MFMIPs and UV–vis absorption spectrum of 4-NP. (C) Fluorescence intensities of CDs-MFNIPs, CDs-MFMIPs and CDs-MIPs over six days. (D) Fluorescence responses of CDs-MFMIPs, CDs-MFNIPs, and CDs-MIPs at different contact times. (E) The fluorescence quenching efficiency of CDs-MFMIPs at different pH values (4–9).

Visualization photographs and fluorescence spectra of (A) CDs-MFMIPs and (B) CDs-MFNIPs. Linear relationship of (C) CDs-MFMIPs and (D) CDs-MFNIPs (4-NP concentrations: 0.05, 0.25, 1.25, 2.5, 5, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50 μM).

(A) Changes in F0/F of CDs-MFMIPs and CDs-MFNIPs caused by 4-NP, 2-NP, 3-NP, NB, HQ, PTBP, 4-EP, PG, and 4-CP (all at 50 μM). (B) Chemical structures of 4-NP and its structural analogs. (C) Effect of interfering ions on the fluorescence intensity of CDs-MFMIPs and CDs-MFMIPs with 4-NP (phenols at 50 μM, interfering ions at 2 mM). (D) Fluorescence intensity (a) and corresponding F0/F (b) for the reusability of CDs-MFMIPs.

(A) Schematic of the CDs-MFMIPs-based visual-sensing platform with a quantitative device. (B) Variation in fluorescence color with different concentrations of 4-NP (UV lamp: 365 nm). (C) Linear plots of Imean versus 4-NP concentrations (4-NP concentrations: 0.1, 0.25, 1.25, 2.5, 5, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50 μM). (D) Comparison of F0/F and Imean values against 4-NP concentrations.
Schematic illustration of the synthesis of CDs-MFMIPs and smartphone-assisted detection of 4-NP.
Determination of 4-NP in actual food samples using the proposed CDs-MFMIPs and HPLC methods. (n = 3).

head.ce:title: A smart imprinted fluorescence sensor of biomass carbon dots based on magnetic covalent organic frameworks for real-time and sensitive detection of 4-nitrophenol in food

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qiqihar University, Qiqihar 161006, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Developing sensitive and portable methods for detecting 4-nitrophenol (4-NP) in food is important for ensuring food safety. Herein, a novel detection system using magnetic covalent organic frameworks (MCOFs) combined with biomass carbon dots (CDs) embedded in molecularly imprinted polymers (CDs-MFMIPs) has been designed specifically for detecting 4-NP in food. The biomass CDs synthesized from green persimmons serve as the fluorescent source, while the MCOFs enhance the response signals. The synergistic effect of the fluorescence response from the biomass CDs and the specificity of imprinting sites enable excellent 4-NP detection. This ultrasensitive fluorescence probe exhibits rapid response, excellent selectivity, magnetic properties, and a wide linear range of 0.05–50 μM, with a low detection limit of 17.44 nM. Furthermore, the smartphone-assisted sensor allows for convenient, visual, and precise 4-NP detection. This approach offers a reliable method and promising applications for onsite detection of 4-NP in food.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: 4-Nitrophenol (4-NP) is a crucial component in industrial production and pharmaceutical manufacturing [1]. However, its complex and costly wastewater treatment makes complete removal challenging. 4-NP enters the food chain via manufacturing wastewater, chemical processing, and agricultural runoff, and it has gained increasing attention in the food industry [2]. Even at low concentrations, 4-NP can cause mortality in aquatic organisms, suppress microbial communities, and induce carcinogenesis in animals [3]. Consequently, the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency has labeled 4-NP as a priority hazardous contaminant [4] and set a maximum concentration limit of 60 ng mL−1 4-NP in drinking water [5,6].

To date, several analytical techniques have been developed to detect 4-NP, including chromatographic [7] and electrochemical methods [8]. While chromatography offers high precision and reliability, its limitations, such as expensive instrumentation, time consumption, and the need for specialized technical expertise, make it insufficient for the rapidly increasing demand for quick and convenient detection [9–11]. Although electrochemical methods provide high sensitivity and accuracy, they are constrained by limited selectivity and are unsuitable for onsite detection. Thus, developing a simple, efficient, sensitive, and real-time detection technique is essential for detecting 4-NP in food samples.

Fluorescence analysis methods are user-friendly [12], sensitive [13], and cost-effective [14]. However, their application is considerably hindered by lack of selectivity. Therefore, developing a fluorescence detection technique that can sensitively and selectively detect 4-NP in food samples is highly desirable and holds promising potential for various applications.

Molecular imprinting polymers (MIPs) feature selective cavities that can specifically bind to templates because of their morphological and functional complementarity [15–17]. For example, Omer et al. [18] created a surface imprinted polymer as a highly specific ratiometric fluorescence probe for ascorbic acid. In recent years, researchers have developed fluorescent molecularly imprinted sensors by integrating fluorescence analysis with molecular imprinting technology for the selective fluorescence detection of 4-NP. For instance, Zhu et al [19] designed a magnetic fluorescent sensor based on MIPs for the selective detection of 4-NP. However, most of these sensing materials rely on QDs synthesized from traditional organic reagents, which often exhibit potential cellular toxicity and may not be suitable for food sample detection. Fortunately, carbon dots (CDs) can be easily prepared from precursors obtained through environmentally friendly “green” methods [20–23]. Compared with QDs, biomass CDs not only demonstrate excellent optical properties but also exhibit good dispersibility, hydrophilicity, and biocompatibility [24,25]. Therefore, incorporating biomass CDs into the development of fluorescent sensors for 4-NP detection is highly significant. However, a challenge remains, i.e., biomass CDs often suffer from severe aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ), which reduces their sensitivity and limits the development of high-sensitivity fluorescent molecularly imprinted sensors.

Covalent organic frameworks (COFs), as novel highly crystalline materials, offer high stability, low density, and large porosity, making them ideal for applications in sensors, adsorption, and drug delivery [26–28]. Incorporating Fe3O4 nanoparticles into COFs creates magnetically responsive COFs (MCOFs), which can simplify sample pretreatments [29]. To address the lack of signal transduction in MCOFs, we propose combining fluorescent molecularly imprinted materials with MCOFs to leverage their respective advantages. Integrating MCOFs into the MIPs system not only provides additional active sites and high porosity, enhancing imprinting site availability and mass transfer rates, but also expands the specific surface area of MIPs. This facilitates the uniform dispersion of CDs nanoparticles within the matrix, thereby addressing the ACQ of CDs and concentrating analytes to improve detection sensitivity [30,31]. To meet onsite real-time inspection requirements, we propose a visual intelligent detection system for 4-NP.

Herein, we developed a novel fluorescence-sensing material that combines MCOFs-supported, CDs-embedded MIPs (CDs-MFMIPs) with high selectivity and sensitivity for the first time. We also constructed a visual and digital platform for the portable, real-time onsite 4-NP detection. In this approach, MCOFs serve as a support for MIPs prepared with inorganic silicate monomers, addressing the ACQ of CDs and improving mass transfer rates and binding efficiency. The synergistic effect of the fluorescence response of biomass CDs and the specificity of the imprinting sites further improves the ability of the nanosensor to specifically recognize 4-NP. The nanosensor demonstrates superior binding efficiency and selectivity for 4-NP and has been successfully used for the onsite detection of 4-NP in actual food samples.

Green persimmons were purchased from a local supermarket in Heilongjiang, China. Hexahydrate ferric chloride (FeCl3·6H2O), sodium acetate (NaAc), sodium citrate (Na3Cit·2H2O), ethylene glycol (EG), 1,3,5-triformylphoroglucinol (Tp), trisodium citrate (C6H5Na3O7·2H2O, Na3Cit), p-phenylenediamine (pa-1), tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS), and (3-aminopropyl) triethoxysilane (APTES) were obtained from Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), 4-NP, 2-nitrophenol (2-NP), 3-nitrophenol (3-NP), nitrobenzene (NB), hydroquinone (HQ), p-tert-butylphenol (PTBP), 4-ethylphenol (4-EP), phloroglucinol (PG) and 4-chlorophenol (4-CP) were purchased from Tianjian Chemical Co., Ltd. (Hefei, China).

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) characterizations were performed using a field-emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM; Hitachi S-4300, Japan), while transmission electron microscopy (TEM; SITACHI H-7650, Japan) was utilized to observe the morphology. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were analyzed using a Bruker D8-FOCUS (Germany). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded using a NEXUS 470 FT-IR spectrometer. Fluorescence measurements were conducted using an LS55 spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, USA).
Carboxylate Fe3O4 was synthesized based on a previous study with slight modifications [32].

The specific process is as follows: 5.4 g of FeCl3·6H2O was ultrasonically dispersed in 80 mL of ethylene glycol solution. Then, 10.8 g of NaAc and 0.9 g of Na3Cit·2H2O were added, and the mixture was magnetically stirred for 2 h. The solution was then transferred to a reaction kettle and heated to 200 °C for 10 h. After the reaction, the product was washed three times with deionized water and ethanol. The resulting black solid was collected through magnetic separation and vacuum-dried at 60 °C to obtain black powder for later use.

MCOFs were synthesized using room-temperature ultrasonication [29]. First, Fe3O4 (0.65 mmol) was dispersed in 30 mL of DMSO. Then, Tp (0.30 mmol) and pa-1 (0.45 mmol) were added, followed by the dropwise addition of 2 mL of glacial acetic acid. The mixture was ultrasonicated for 5 min and then reacted at room temperature for 30 min. The product was collected via magnetic separation, washed with methanol, and dried. Finally, the dried product was ground into a uniform powder.

Fifty grams of green persimmons were washed, minced, and mixed with 100 mL of ultrapure water. After ultrasonic treatment at 30 °C for 20 min, the mixture was filtered to remove large particles. The filtrate was further filtered through filter paper to obtain green persimmon extract. The extract was added to a 100 mL polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) lined hydrothermal synthesis kettle and placed in a high-temperature oven at 180 °C for 4 h. After centrifugation, the resulting brown solution was dialyzed in a dialysis bag for 48 h. Finally, CDs were obtained by filtering the dialyzed dispersion through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane again and freeze-drying.

4-NP (1 mmol), APTES (4 mmol), and biomass CDs (25 mg) were ultrasonically dispersed in 20 mL ethanol. After being kept in the dark for 1 h, MCOFs (100 mg) were added and stirred continuously for 1 h at room temperature. Then, 8 mmol of TEOS (crosslinking agent) and 1 mL of NH3·H2O (1 mol L–1) were added, and the reaction proceeded for 12 h at 60 °C. The product was washed several times with methanol and acetic acid (9:1, v/v) until 4-NP was no longer detectable. Non-molecularly imprinted polymers (CDs-MFNIPs) and conventional polymers (CDs-MIPs) were prepared using the same process as CDs-MFMIPs without adding 4-NP and MCOFs, respectively.

Stock solutions of CDs-MFMIPs, CDs-MFNIPs, and CDs-MIPs were prepared by dispersing polymer nanoparticles (1 mg) in a 5 mL buffer solution (pH = 8). Fluorescence values at the maximum emission wavelength with and without the presence of 4-NP were denoted as F0 and F, respectively. Both the incident and exit slits were set to 5 nm, and the excitation wavelength was adjusted to 365 nm. Fluorescence spectra were recorded from 400 to 600 nm. The entire process was repeated thrice, and the average values were used to plot the relationship between fluorescence intensity and varying concentrations of 4-NP (0.05–50 μM). Additionally, the color of the mixture was observed under a 365 nm UV lamp.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Smartphone based sensing device for visual 4-NP detection
ce:section.ce:para: To ensure that external influences, such as the excitation beam and distance, do not interfere with the practical operation of the sensor device, we precisely installed the optical components. The main components of this intelligent device are shown in Fig. S1. The cuvette and a 365 nm UV lamp, which was powered by a portable power bank, were fixed within a light-proof cassette. A filter (400 nm) was placed between the cuvette and the smartphone to eliminate interference from the excitation light beam. A smartphone was used to capture fluorescent color images.

The app (Color Picker) was employed to convert the fluorescence color change into RGB values. Grayscale values (I) were calculated based on the formula I = 0.3 R + 0.59 G + 0.11B [33]. To further improve the detection accuracy, the mean value (Imean), is given by the equation Imean = (Iblank − Itest)/Iblank. A linear relationship between the Imean and the concentration of 4-NP was established to enable quantitative detection of 4-NP [34].

Tap water and fresh animal-origin foods (fish and shrimp purchased from the local supermarket) were used as real samples to demonstrate the effectiveness of the CDs-MFMIPs sensors in detecting 4-NP. The samples were stored in a refrigerator and tested using standard addition methods.

Meat samples (fish and shrimp) were homogenized and mixed with solutions containing different 4-NP concentrations (0, 50, 100 and 200 nmol). After 15 min, the mixture was subjected to ultrasonic treatment for 15 min. The resulting mixture was then dispersed into buffer solutions (pH = 8, totaling 20 mL, divided into 4 aliquots). After centrifugation, the supernatant was collected and filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane. The filtrate was then used for fluorescence analysis. Tap water was processed similarly to meat samples, excluding the homogenization step. Finally, the 4-NP content in each treated sample was determined using previously described methods and procedures.

The content of 4-NP was analyzed using an Elite HPLC equipped with a C18 column (size 4.6 mm × 200 mm). The UV detector was set to a wavelength of 312 nm, and the mobile phase consisted of water and methanol (7:3, v/v) at a flow rate of 1.0 mL min−1.

As shown in Scheme 1 , the –COOH groups on the Fe3O4 nanoparticles enhance their dispersion stability in solution, facilitating the in situ growth of the COF shell [35]. This shell is synthesized using Tp and pa-1 as organic ligands on the surface of the Fe3O4 nanoparticles. Subsequently, fluorescent biomass CDs were prepared using a hydrothermal method with green persimmon as the precursor. The fluorescence quantum yield of CDs was 10.45 %, which was measured using quinine sulfate as a fluorescence standard. These biomass CDs primarily consist of a sp2 hybridized conjugated carbon core surrounded by numerous nitrogen and oxygen functional groups. The CDs-MFMIPs was prepared by the hydrolysis and crosslinking of the APTES and TEOS. The process allows the incorporation of CDs into the network followed by attachment onto the surface of the MCOFs and the fluorescent CDs are able to be integrated into the Si–O–Si framework network, which in turn forms molecular imprinting film deposited on the MCOFs surface. After the removal of 4-NP, the final product, CDs-MFMIPs, was obtained. This material features imprinted cavities with complementary functional groups, shapes, and sizes to 4-NP.

The introduction of MCOFs provides crucial support to prevent the ACQ of biomass CDs and enhances the specific recognition of imprinting sites for 4-NP. Using eco-friendly biomass CDs as the fluorescent signal and the MIPs layer for specific recognition, the nanosensor retains the sensitive fluorescent response of the CDs while significantly improving selectivity. Consequently, the synthesized fluorescent nanosensor presents an innovative approach for the efficient and eco-friendly detection of 4-NP in food. As detailed in Table S1–S3, the significant synthetic conditions for optimizing the performance of CDs-MFMIPs for 4-NP sensing were thoroughly optimized.

Morphological studies using SEM and TEM (Fig. 1 A) reveal that the Fe3O4 nanoparticles are spherical, well-dispersed, and possess a rough surface. In the SEM and TEM images of the MCOFs (Fig. 1B), it is evident that the surface of the Fe3O4 nanoparticles is covered with a loosely ordered shell approximately 10 nm thick. The introduction of Fe3O4 nanoparticles enhances the morphology of the COFs. After polymerization, the CDs-MFMIPs shown in Fig. 1C also exhibit uniform sphericity, with an imprinting layer approximately 27 nm thick. Fig. 1D shows the TEM image of the CDs, which are spherical, well-dispersed, and uniformly sized, with dimensions of approximately 3.07 ± 0.01 nm (insert in Fig. 1D).

As demonstrated in the XRD spectrum shown in Fig. 2 A, the diffraction peaks of Fe3O4 (a) appear at 2θ = 30.08°, 35.42°, 43.04°, 53.38°, 56.92° and 62.44°, which correspond well with previous reports [29], indicating the successful preparation of Fe3O4. In MCOFs (b), the crystal configuration of Fe3O4 remains stable, however, the diffraction peak near 4.7° may be associated with low crystallinity of Tppa-1 [36]. The XRD pattern of CDs (c) displays a diffraction peak centered at about 22.6°, indicating the graphitic structure of the as-synthesized CDs [37]. Notably, in CDs-MFMIPs (d), we observe an amorphous diffraction peak associated with biomass CDs, confirming that the biomass CDs are embedded into the composite.

Fig. 2B (a) shows the infrared spectrum of CDs, where the broad absorption peak at approximately 3436 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibrations of the –OH and N–H groups. The peaks at 2925 cm−1, 1400 cm−1 and 1068 cm−1 are attributed to C–H, C–N, and C–O stretching vibrations, respectively, demonstrating the successful formation of biomass CDs [38,39]. Fig. 2B (b) shows the infrared spectrum of Fe3O4, where the prominent absorption peak at 589 cm−1 corresponds to the Fe–O–Fe stretching vibrations. The absorption peak at 1625 cm−1 is associated with the stretching vibrations of C = O, confirming the presence of a carboxyl group on Fe3O4 [35,41]. As shown in Fig. 2B (c), the infrared spectrum of MCOFs exhibits characteristic peaks at 1582 and 1256 cm−1, representing the stretching vibrations of C = C and C–N within the covalent organic framework [40]. This suggests the successful coating of the Tppa-1 shell onto the Fe3O4 surface through Schiff base condensation. Fig. 2B (d) shows that CDs-MFMIPs retain the characteristic peaks of MCOFs while also displaying absorption bands at 1092 cm−1 (Si–O–Si), indicating that the imprinting layer was formed by the hydrolysis and condensation reactions of APTES and TEOS [26,41,42]. Notably, Fig. 2B (e) shows the polymer rebinding with 4-NP, and the absorption peak observed at 854 cm−1 is caused by the para-substitution of the benzene ring in 4-NP, confirming the successful incorporation of 4-NP into the imprinting layer [19].

VSM measurements reveal that the hysteresis loops shown in Fig. 2C exhibit typical paramagnetic characteristics. The saturation magnetization values of Fe3O4, MCOFs, and CDs-MFMIPs are recorded as 63.2, 43.6 and 15.8 emu/g, respectively. The sequential decrease in the saturation magnetization values is attributed to the presence of the Tppa-1 shell and the MIPs layer. The paramagnetic properties of CDs-MFMIPs ensure a highly sensitive response in magnetic fields, allowing for sample separation within 15 s without affecting the transparency of the sample solution (insert in Fig. 2C).

The TGA results of CDs-MFMIPs and MCOFs are shown in Fig. 2D. For MCOFs, a weight loss of approximately 5 % from 25 °C to 150 °C is attributed to the evaporation of solvent (water). From 150 °C to 760 °C, MCOFs exhibit a 23.24 % weight loss, primarily due to the decomposition of framework. In comparison, CDs-MFMIPs demonstrate a greater weight loss, which is due to the decomposition of the imprinted layer at high temperatures. These results confirm the successful polymerization of MIPs onto the MCOFs surface.

To analyze the chemical composition of the nanoparticles, we used X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The XPS analysis of Fe3O4 nanoparticles reveals the presence of Fe (43.42 %), O (45.30 %), and C (11.28 %) (Table S4). As shown in Fig. 2E, Fe3O4 displays distinct Fe 2p and O 1 s peaks, with the C 1 s peak corresponding to –COOH groups. The amount of C rises owning to the COFs shell, and the N 1 s peak appears with a nitrogen content of 10.83 %. The full-scan XPS spectrum of CDs-MFMIPs shows characteristic peaks at 102.98, 284.80, 399.40, 531.14 and 710.56 eV, corresponding to Si 2p, C 1 s, N 1 s, O 1 s, and Fe 2p, respectively (Fig. 2E). The presence of Si 2 s and Si 2p peaks confirms successful formation of the imprinting layer [42]. The attachment of imprinting layer enabled the appearances of more O elements (46.92 %) and less Fe elements (1.74 %) [43]. The C 1 s high-resolution XPS spectrum (Fig. 2F) shows peaks at 288.48 eV (C = O), 285.98 eV (C–N/C–O), and 284.80 eV (C–C/C = C). In addition, the peak in the Si 2p spectrum (Fig. S2) can be attributed to the Si–O–Si (102.98 eV) bond, which present the consistency to the characterization of the FT-IR spectrum, suggesting the successful modification of the imprinting layer in MCOFs.

First, we evaluated the feasibility of the fluorescent sensor for 4-NP detection. As shown in Fig. 3 A, FL spectrum of MCOFs was basically a straight line, manifesting that MCOFs had a negligible effect on the luminescence properties of the composites. After removing the target 4-NP, the FL intensity of CDs-MFMIPs recovered to nearly that of CDs-MFNIPs, suggesting the target had been removed from CDs-MFMIPs. The presence of template molecules significantly quenched the fluorescence intensity of CDs-MFMIPs. At an excitation wavelength of 365 nm, CDs-MFMIPs exhibited more pronounced fluorescence changes compared with CDs-MFNIPs and CDs-MIPs, indicating stronger affinity for 4-NP.

To clarify the mechanism behind the fluorescence quenching of CDs-MFMIPs by 4-NP, we measured the UV–visible (vis) absorption spectrum of 4-NP and the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of CDs-MFMIPs (Fig. 3B). The lack of overlap between the UV–vis absorption spectrum of 4-NP and the fluorescence emission spectrum of CDs-MFMIPs rules out fluorescence resonance energy transfer as the quenching mechanism. However, the partial overlap between the fluorescence excitation spectrum of CDs-MFMIPs and the UV–vis absorption spectrum of 4-NP suggests the presence of an inner filter effect (IFE) between CDs-MFMIPs and the template molecule 4-NP. To further investigate the quenching mechanism, we examined the fluorescence lifetimes of the system with and without the template molecule (Fig. S3). After binding with 4-NP, the fluorescence lifetime of the sensing system exhibited a slight change from 5.04 to 4.98 ns, confirming that the fluorescence quenching caused by 4-NP is primarily attributed to IFE [12].

Excellent fluorescence stability is essential for the practical application of CDs-MFMIPs. From Fig. 3C, it can be seen the fluorescence emission intensity of CDs-MIPs gradually decreased within six days. In contrast, CDs-MFNIPs and CDs-MFMIPs maintained stable fluorescence during this period, demonstrating superior physical and chemical stability, which can be attributed to the presence of MCOFs as a support material, which prevents the self-quenching of biomass CDs.

The fluorescence response (F0/F) of the CDs-MFMIPs to 4-NP increased rapidly with the time (Fig. 3D) and reached equilibrium within 1 min, which was selected as the optimal adsorption time. Notably, CDs-MFMIPs exhibited a significantly faster fluorescence response than CDs-MIPs. This improvement can be attributed to the introduction of MCOFs, which enhanced the accessibility of the surface imprinting sites for 4-NP, resulting in a higher mass transfer rate and adsorption capacity. Thus, MCOFs, as an innovative support material, offer a promising approach for developing molecularly imprinted fluorescent nanosensors with high binding and rapid mass transfer capabilities.

As shown in Fig. 3E, the pH effect on the fluorescence quenching ability of the CDs-MFMIPs sensor in the presence of 4-NP solution (50 μM) was investigated in the range of 4–9. The sensing system achieved the highest quenching efficiency at pH = 8. Thus, pH = 8 was selected as the optimal condition for subsequent fluorescence sensing.

To study the fluorescence emission characteristics of the sensing system, we tested the prepared fluorescent materials (CDs-MFMIPs and CDs-MFNIPs) in solutions containing various 4-NP concentrations. As shown in Fig. 4 A and B, the fluorescence intensities of both polymers decreased with increasing 4-NP concentration. However, CDs-MFMIPs exhibited more pronounced changes in fluorescence color and higher fluorescence quenching efficiency than CDs-MFNIPs at the same concentration. This result is attributed to the presence of cavities in CDs-MFMIPs that are well-matched to the template molecules. Within a concentration range of 0.05–50 μM, there is a linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity of CDs-MFMIPs and the 4-NP concentration, as illustrated in Fig. 4C. The limit of detection (LOD), calculated using the standard 3σ/S method [44,45], is 17.44 nM. The linear equations for CDs-MFMIPs and CDs-MFNIPs are F0/F = 0.0179 C4-NP + 1.072, (R2 = 0.9975) and F0/F = 0.0023 C4-NP + 1.005, (R2 = 0.9906), respectively (Fig. 4D). Comparing the slopes of these equations highlights the superior fluorescence response capability of CDs-MFMIPs for 4-NP. The imprinting factor (IF = 7.7), a key indicator of selectivity, further demonstrates the excellent recognition capabilities of CDs-MFMIPs for 4-NP.

To validate the superior fluorescent properties of the proposed CDs-MFMIPs probe, we compared its analytical performance with that of CDs-MIPs in a control experiment (Fig. S4). The fluorescence response of CDs-MIPs to 4-NP displayed a linear correlation within the range of 0.15–40 μM. However, the LOD for CDs-MFMIPs was significantly lower (17.44 nM) than that for CDs-MIPs (84.68 nM). This improved sensitivity is attributed to the incorporation of MCOFs, which create numerous imprinting sites on the nanosensor surface and enhance the recognition of CDs-MFMIPs to 4-NP. Additionally, MCOFs concentrate the analytes, leading to signal amplification. Overall, the comparative analysis confirms that the proposed nanosensor exhibits superior sensitivity for 4-NP detection.

To evaluate the selectivity of the fluorescent probe for 4-NP, we tested several structural analogs. As shown in Fig. 5 A, the fluorescence response (F0/F) of CDs-MFMIPs to 4-NP was significantly greater than that of the other analogs. The response is due to the combined effect of the fluorescence recognition properties of biomass CDs and the specificity of the imprinting sites. The fluorescence response of CDs-MFMIPs to HQ, PTBP, 4-EP, PG, and 4-CP showed minimal changes, likely because these analogs lack nitro groups (Fig. 5B) [19]. However, CDs-MFMIPs demonstrated much higher fluorescence quenching efficiencies for 4-NP than for 2-NP, 3-NP, and NB. This result confirms that the recognition sites in the imprint layer are well-matched to the template molecules in terms of size, morphology, and functional groups, indicating excellent specificity for 4-NP. In contrast, CDs-MFNIPs exhibited a lower fluorescence response (F0/F) to 4-NP than CDs-MFMIPs because of the absence of specific recognition sites.

Additionally, to assess the specificity of the probe for 4-NP in complex food samples, we conducted selectivity experiments with common interfering ions (Na+, Cl−, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, SO4 2−, CO3 2−, and HCO3 −). As shown in Fig. 5C, CDs-MFMIPs displayed minimal fluorescence fluctuations in the presence of these ions compared with the blank group. However, when 4-NP was added, the sensor system exhibited significant fluorescence quenching. This indicates the excellent anti-interference properties of the probe and its suitability for accurate identification of 4-NP in real samples.

To evaluate the reusability of the developed fluorescent probe, we monitored the changes in the fluorescence of CDs-MFMIPs over six cycles of adsorption and desorption. As shown in Fig. 5D, the fluorescence intensity of CDs-MFMIPs remained largely unchanged throughout the cycles. Even after six cycles, CDs-MFMIPs retained excellent performance for 4-NP. After elution of 4-NP, the fluorescence intensity was restored, and the probe continued to exhibit its characteristic fluorescence with a peak position at 450 nm (Fig. S5). These results demonstrate the outstanding reusability of CDs-MFMIPs.

The presence of 4-NP in food samples (tap water, fish and shrimp meat) was detected using the proposed fluorescence analysis and HPLC-UV methods. As shown in Table 1 , recoveries with the CDs-MFMIPs probe ranged from 93.80 % to 103.05 % with a relative standard deviation (RSD) of less than 4.64 %, which was highly consistent with those obtained using HPLC-UV. These findings demonstrate that the CDs-MFMIPs fluorescent probe can accurately and effectively detect 4-NP in real food samples.

The quantitative analysis of 4-NP using smart portable devices is still limited; thus, we compared our method with previously developed fluorescence sensing methods. As shown in Table S5, the CDs-MFMIPs developed in this study exhibit excellent sensitivity with a low LOD. The notable advantages of CDs-MFMIPs include the following: (I) Enhanced sensitivity: MCOFs improve the adsorption performance and mass transfer rate while preventing self-quenching of biomass CDs, improving the sensitivity of fluorescent nanosensors; (II) Rapid response: CDs-MFMIPs provide a quick fluorescent response within 1 min; (III) Higher IF value: The IF value of 7.7 is higher than previous reports due to the double recognition mechanism; (IV) Environmental safety: Biomass CDs provide an eco-friendly fluorescent signal suitable for food detection, and the magnetic response of CDs-MFMIPs simplifies the detection process and facilitates easy recycling; (V) Onsite visual inspection: The smartphone-assisted fluorescent sensing platform simplifies the detection process, eliminates the need for large instruments, and enables real-time onsite monitoring. Overall, our fluorescent sensing strategy shows great promise for the rapid, sensitive, real-time detection of 4-NP in the food industry.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Smartphone-assisted portable device for 4-NP detection in food
ce:section.ce:para: Although the CDs-MFMIPs-based fluorescence method offers excellent sensitivity for detecting 4-NP in food, bulky fluorometers are often unsuitable for onsite analysis and real-time monitoring. In addition, detecting subtle color changes with the naked eye is challenging. To address these limitations, we developed an intelligent detection platform using CDs-MFMIPs with a custom optical setup assisted by a smartphone. The optical components were fixed to ensure a dark environment for accurate detection. As shown in Fig. 6 A, the sample is placed in the cuvette, and the smartphone reads and analyzes the fluorescence signals, extracts the RGB values, and converts them to the corresponding Imean values. This setup allows for practical onsite and real-time monitoring without specialized equipment. Images (Fig. 6B) show a continuous color change from light blue to dark blue with increasing 4-NP concentration. As shown in Fig. 6C, the Imean values exhibited a good linear relationship with 4-NP concentrations of 0.1–50 μM, with the corresponding linear equation described by Imean = 0.0162 C4-NP + 0.117 (R2 = 0.9957). The LOD, calculated using the 3σ/S method, was 30.24 nM. This approach is highly favorable for meeting safety regulations and food quality analysis requirements. Additionally, as shown in Fig. 6D, the slope of the linear relationship between Imean and C4-NP closely matches that of F0/F measured with the fluorescence sensor, indicating strong potential of the smart sensing platform for precise onsite analysis of 4-NP in food.

Similarly, we used the smartphone-integrated sensing platform to analyze actual samples. The recovery rates were 93.72 %-102.58 % with RSD of ≤ 4.13 %, as shown in Table S6, which are in good agreement with the results obtained using both the CDs-MFMIPs-based fluorescence method and HPLC. Therefore, the smartphone-integrated fluorescence-sensing platform shows great potential for widespread application in 4-NP detection in food.

Herein, we successfully developed a novel fluorescent sensor for detecting 4-NP in food, leveraging the combined advantages of MCOFs, biomass CDs, and MIPs. The CDs-MFMIPs sensor demonstrated a rapid fluorescent response within 1 min, a wide line range (0.05–50 μM), highly selective recognition (IF = 7.7), and ultrasensitivity (LOD = 17.44 nM) for 4-NP. The favorable application of this fluorescent sensor was confirmed by testing fresh animal-origin food samples. Notably, by integrating CDs-MFMIPs with a smartphone-assisted fluorescence-sensing device, we created a portable fluorescence-sensing platform. This sensing platform effectively mitigates environmental fluctuations, thus meeting the requirements for precise onsite 4-NP analysis in food. The practicality of this fluorescence-sensing platform was further validated by recovery rates of 93.72 %-102.58 %. This study not only facilitates the quantitative determination of 4-NP for food safety but also offers a new approach for developing onsite portable sensor platforms for food inspection.

Chen Yan: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Data curation. Shuang Han: Writing – review & editing, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Ruonan Sun: Validation. Le Zhao: Validation. Xin Zhang: Methodology. Xinyuan Zhang: Methodology. Yuan Wang: Formal analysis. Minghui Zhai: Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was funded by the Program for Young Talents of Basic Research in Universities of Heilongjiang Province (YQJH2023101), Ecological Environment Protection Project of Heilongjiang Province (HST2023QT002), Innovation Incentive Project of Qiqihar Technology Program (CSFGG-2023283) and the Fundamental Research Funds in Heilongjiang Provincial Universities (No: 135409101).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.111949.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
